header.stance: Payroll resilience restored a real-yield-led Treasury bear flattening at the NY close
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

Friday’s payroll surprise ended the prior close’s conditional relief and restored a policy-led bear flattening as the dominant Treasury regime. Nonfarm payrolls increased 172,000, topping every forecast, and prior months were revised higher. The result converted tentative labor cooling into renewed confidence that nominal demand can tolerate restrictive policy. The two-year yield closed at 4.17% and the 10-year at 4.55%, with the front end under the greater pressure. That pattern reopened the tightening path that Thursday’s lower oil and softer labor-cost signals had only temporarily restrained.

The composition was more important than the size of the nominal move. The 10-year real yield rose to 2.19%, accounting for the full increase in the corresponding nominal yield because breakeven inflation was unchanged. Investors demanded more real compensation for resilient activity and a potentially higher policy rate; they did not mark up long-horizon inflation compensation again. The strongest causal channel therefore ran from labor resilience into Federal Reserve pricing and real discount rates, with the belly and front end absorbing most of the adjustment.

Cross assets reinforced that interpretation while revealing a second source of stress. Oil declined, so a fresh energy-price shock could not explain the Treasury selloff. Technology shares instead suffered a sharp valuation reset, the dollar strengthened, gold fell and volatility jumped. Higher real yields were transmitted directly into long-duration equity valuations, though profit-taking, financing concerns and company news amplified the technology move. The combination looked like a repricing of the cost of capital, not a conventional flight toward government bonds.

Funding markets remained more resilient than headline risk assets. Short bill yields were calm, a completed Google-linked municipal transaction tightened in secondary trading, and a very large chip-financing package reached final terms across several layers of risk. Credit-fund prices fell, yet completed deals showed that capital was still available when structures allocated collateral, guarantees and loss exposure clearly. Global policy evidence added another constraint: India held rates while raising its inflation forecast, and later European debate kept an ECB increase in view. The central conclusion from the prior session has therefore changed in price but not in regime: relief proved fragile, the real-rate floor strengthened, and selective financing access prevented a rates shock from becoming a generalized funding break.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury yields rose across every benchmark maturity, with losses diminishing toward the long end. The two-year increased 12 basis points to 4.17%, the five-year added 11 to 4.29%, the 10-year rose eight to 4.55%, and the 30-year climbed four to 5.01%. The 2s10s slope narrowed four basis points to 38, while 2s30s flattened eight to 84. This was a front-loaded bear flattening: policy expectations and the medium-term real-rate path moved more abruptly than long-run inflation or fiscal compensation.

Curvature located the pressure in the front half without making the five-year sector uniquely weak. The 2s5s10s butterfly rose two basis points to minus 14, leaving the five-year relatively rich to its wings even after its large outright selloff. The 2s10s30s butterfly held at minus eight. Over the week, two- and five-year yields had risen 19 and 16 basis points, against 10 at 10 years and only two at 30 years. The curve was carrying a cumulative reassessment of the Fed path, not a one-day long-bond supply premium.

Inflation-linked securities gave the cleanest decomposition. The five-year real yield rose 11 basis points to 1.81%, the seven-year increased nine to 1.99%, and the 10-year added eight to 2.19%. The 20- and 30-year real yields rose five and three basis points, to 2.55% and 2.74%. Ten-year breakeven inflation stayed at 2.36% on the day and was lower over the preceding week and month. The nominal selloff therefore reflected a higher real discount-rate structure, with the same declining sensitivity by maturity visible in both nominal and inflation-protected bonds.

The employment report supplied the catalyst and enough breadth to overturn Thursday’s labor relief. Payrolls increased 172,000 in May after upward revisions to the prior two months, and the unemployment rate held at 4.3%. Leisure and hospitality added 70,000 positions, its strongest contribution in more than three years, while labor-force participation stayed at 61.8%. The details did not show an economy losing hiring momentum quickly enough to offset above-target inflation. Treasuries sold off immediately after the release, and the two-year’s intraday rise was the largest in more than a year.

Market pricing translated that resilience into a substantially firmer policy path. Interest-rate swaps fully embedded a quarter-point increase by the December meeting and assigned roughly a 60% chance to an October move. The June meeting remained associated with a hold, but the burden shifted toward the Fed explaining why policy should retain any easing bias. The new chairman’s first meeting now sits between a labor market that appears to be recovering and inflation already elevated by energy and supply disruptions.

Policy uncertainty did not disappear simply because the market converged on a hike. Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said an increase may soon be appropriate with employment near balance. Citi continued to expect three quarter-point reductions late in the year, arguing that labor would soften, while Goldman removed its December cut forecast yet still regarded an increase as unlikely. Those disagreements identify the counterforce: one strong payroll report can raise the real-rate hurdle decisively, but the persistence of labor strength and inflation pass-through still has to be established.

The 15:00 ET consumer-credit release added a late demand signal without driving the morning repricing. April borrowing increased by $20.73 billion, above the $17.80 billion consensus and after a revised $22.23 billion March gain. Total credit grew at a 4.8% annual rate, with revolving balances up 10.4% and nonrevolving credit up 2.9%. Faster card borrowing can accompany resilient consumption or greater household strain, so it reinforces nominal-demand uncertainty more clearly than it establishes stronger household balance sheets.

Immediate cash conditions remained orderly beneath the coupon selloff. Four-, six- and eight-week coupon-equivalent bill yields were unchanged at 3.68%, 3.68% and 3.69%. The 13-week yield also held at 3.71%, while 17-week, 26-week and 52-week yields rose two, three and five basis points. The adjustment increased with maturity across bills, mirroring policy repricing farther out, while the shortest instruments showed no common liquidity disturbance.

Announced Treasury operations now create the next absorption sequence, not evidence about auction quality on Friday. The calendar contains $89 billion of 13-week bills and $77 billion of 26-week bills on June 8, followed by $65 billion of six-week and $50 billion of 52-week bills on June 9. Coupon supply comprises $58 billion at three years, a $39 billion 10-year reopening and a $22 billion 30-year reopening. Liquidity-support and cash-management buybacks can improve trading in eligible securities, but they do not remove the need for investors to absorb the new coupon duration.

Options activity supplied a limited challenge to the closing consensus. Demand appeared for July 10-year calls targeting a yield decline toward 4.4% over a period spanning inflation data and the Fed meeting. That hedge is consistent with a market aware that payrolls may have pushed real yields too far if price data or policy language disappoint hawkish expectations. The close nonetheless placed the burden on softer evidence: flat breakevens, declining oil and alternative economist forecasts restrained the selloff’s interpretation, but none displaced its observed front-end and real-yield leadership.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit total returns weakened alongside Treasuries and equities. LQD fell 0.6247%, HYG declined 0.5011%, and JNK lost 0.4368%. The larger duration exposure of investment grade contributed to its underperformance, while the risk-off equity session weighed across lower-quality funds. These listed returns describe a broad deterioration in valuation and risk tone; completed financing terms show that access remained open and highly selective.

The roughly $1 billion California prepaid-energy transaction linked to Alphabet offered the clearest public-market absorption signal. California Community Choice Financing Authority issued the bonds for Pioneer Community Energy, with Alphabet serving as the financial intermediary and Goldman Sachs underwriting. The largest portion priced 95 basis points above its benchmark. It subsequently changed hands at tighter levels, indicating that investors found the structure and counterparty attractive even as government yields rose.

Demand was particularly visible in the 2035 maturity. Trading in that security reached $492 million by Friday afternoon, at an average spread of 89 basis points and with some transactions as tight as 74. The secondary performance matters because it followed an actual completed sale, unlike an announced financing target. It also shows how a major technology counterparty can broaden the prepaid-energy buyer base while delivering long-term electricity savings to the participating utility.

Private credit absorbed a much larger AI infrastructure requirement through a carefully tiered structure. Apollo and Blackstone finalized $35 billion of debt to finance Google-designed chips that Anthropic will lease through a special-purpose vehicle. About half of the package was syndicated. A $6 billion senior tranche priced one percentage point over Treasuries, and $24 billion of additional senior notes priced at par with a 5.75% coupon. Both layers carried private mid-investment-grade ratings and support from Broadcom.

Loss allocation explained the gap to the junior debt. The $4.5 billion B tranche had no Broadcom backing and priced at par with an 8.5% coupon. For the senior layers, Broadcom agreed to cover any shortfall if a defaulted lease led to chip sales that failed to repay investors. The structure transferred residual-value risk away from senior buyers, allowing insurers and banks to fund long-lived computing assets at a lower cost. It also concentrated performance risk in Anthropic’s lease payments, chip values and the unguaranteed tranche.

The two transactions show why AI financing can expand even during a real-yield shock. Municipal investors accepted a new prepaid-energy counterparty, while private lenders differentiated between guaranteed senior claims and exposed junior claims. Capital remained available because documentation specified the payment waterfall and support mechanism. The scale is also a duration consideration: technology investment is drawing on municipal, private-credit and insurance balance sheets at the same time that Treasury issuance remains heavy.

A later development in Brazilian corporate credit showed the other end of that allocation process. After the US close, Raizen disclosed an out-of-court agreement covering about 65 billion reais, or $12.6 billion, of debt, supported by creditors holding 75.5% of the obligations in the plan. The proposal converts 45% into equity and exchanges the remaining 55% for new debt. This is a transfer of enterprise risk and ownership, not evidence of routine refinancing access, and it did not explain Friday’s broad fund moves.

Weekend evidence from Japan added a slower-moving refinancing channel. Debt among reporting Nikkei 225 companies reached ¥678 trillion for the fiscal year through March, 4.6% more than a year earlier, as acquisitions, investment and shareholder distributions consumed cash. Nippon Steel’s borrowing doubled around its $14 billion acquisition of United States Steel, contributing to an S&P downgrade. Rising domestic rates will hit weaker industrial credits first, and loan growth that outruns deposits can push more borrowers toward bond markets even as their interest burden rises.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

India’s June decision preserved policy restraint without adding an immediate rate increase. The Reserve Bank of India unanimously held the repo rate at 5.25%, retained a neutral stance and kept the standing deposit facility at 5.00%, with the marginal standing facility and Bank Rate at 5.50%. More consequentially, it raised its fiscal-year inflation forecast to 5.1% from 4.6%. An unchanged rate accompanied by a higher price path limits the scope for near-term duration relief in an energy-importing economy and reinforces competition for global front-end capital.

Taiwan’s May price data showed a related pipeline problem. Consumer inflation accelerated to 2.20% year on year from 1.74%, despite a smaller monthly increase. Producer prices were 14.11% higher than a year earlier, up from 8.54%, and US-dollar import prices rose 17.21%. Currency translation and imported inputs can sustain local rate sensitivity even when monthly producer prices soften. The release broadens the Asian inflation signal without establishing a synchronized policy response.

Europe’s debate combined faster inflation with a weak growth counterweight. Euro-area consumer inflation reached 3.2% in May, and weekend pricing favored an ECB increase at the June 11 meeting with substantial additional tightening embedded through year-end. The one-year, one-year inflation swap had retreated to 2.12% from an April peak of 2.40%, however. Near-term policy pricing remained hawkish, while medium-term compensation no longer showed the same intensity as at the height of the energy shock.

That divergence creates a maturity-specific global transmission. Some large asset managers expected one ECB increase at most because euro-area activity remained weak, and revised data showed first-quarter output contracting. A limited credibility move could keep pressure concentrated at the euro front end and leave room for intermediate bonds to recover. A sequence of increases would raise the relative return needed across sovereign curves and compete more directly with Treasuries for global duration demand.

Fiscal risk remained separate from the policy-rate argument. After Friday’s US cutoff, Morningstar DBRS lowered Austria to AA (high) from AAA, citing a slowly worsening debt trajectory and persistent deficits. The government aims to bring the deficit below 3% of GDP by 2028, while elevated energy and labor costs weigh on growth. Austria’s 10-year bonds yielded 24 basis points more than comparable German debt, and the limited immediate reaction suggested the downgrade formalized an existing fiscal concern more than it created a new regional shock.

Together, these developments keep global duration from moving as a single trade. India’s hold carried hawkish information through the forecast, Taiwan’s pipeline prices raised local pass-through risk, and Europe paired prospective tightening with recession-sensitive dissent. Austria added issuer-specific fiscal differentiation. For Treasuries, the common mechanism is a higher global clearing rate and less confidence that weak growth automatically produces easing, even though country-level growth and inflation outcomes still constrain how far each tightening cycle can extend.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market debt faced a difficult US close. EMB declined 0.7284% as the dollar index rose 0.6639%, combining higher Treasury discount rates with a tighter external-currency backdrop. The fund move included duration and broad risk effects and does not describe every sovereign spread. Country developments after the close showed why external buffers, fiscal credibility and liability structure matter more than a single beta measure.

Colombia’s inflation release later on Friday strengthened the local tightening case. Annual consumer inflation accelerated to 5.84% from 5.68%, monthly prices rose 0.47%, and a core measure reached 5.79%. The result was close to the 5.9% median forecast, yet the direction matters after the central bank unexpectedly held its rate at 11.25% in April amid political pressure on the board. Persistent core inflation raises the cost of extending duration and increases the chance that policy tightening returns.

Across larger emerging markets, the long end carries more than geopolitical risk. Yields in South Korea, Brazil, South Africa and Mexico had climbed more than 40 basis points since the Iran war began. Inflation expectations explained much of the rise, but real yields also increased as fiscal support for fuel and food strained budgets. Lower oil after a peace agreement could benefit front ends quickly; long maturities would still have to absorb defense spending, energy-resilience investment, AI capital requirements and weaker fiscal positions.

Brazil and South Korea illustrate distinct forms of that inertia. Brazilian benchmark yields remained above 14% as gross public debt exceeded 80% of GDP and growth stayed firm. Korea’s 10-year yield hovered near a two-year high of 4.25%, with supplementary-budget issuance adding to inflation and semiconductor-led demand. Both cases make long-duration performance dependent on fiscal supply and real-rate compensation, even if energy prices retreat.

Indonesia’s weekend intervention exposed a more immediate confidence problem. The rupiah had fallen about 8% during the year and breached 18,000 per dollar, while foreign investors had withdrawn a net $422 million from bonds. Authorities supported the market sufficiently to limit the decline in 10-year debt and pledged more liquidity. Bank Indonesia also proposed paying a higher rate on government deposits held at the central bank, aiming to offset some of the sovereign’s rising interest burden.

The Indonesian measure can support yields and government cash flow only if its scale, remuneration and issuance effects are clear. Uncertainty about those terms risks blurring fiscal and monetary responsibilities when higher energy subsidies are already straining the budget. The contrast with Colombia is instructive: one market faces renewed inflation and political pressure on the reaction function; the other faces currency weakness and credibility questions around coordinated intervention. Both transmit into duration through a higher required risk premium.

China’s end-May reserve data supplied a partial regional buffer. Official foreign-exchange reserves rose by $31.7 billion to $3.4422 trillion, a 0.93% monthly increase. The foreign-exchange authority attributed the change mainly to currency conversion and global asset-price movements, so the increase should not be treated as a direct measure of intervention or capital inflow. It nevertheless leaves a large gross reserve stock available to moderate external volatility, distinguishing China’s liquidity position from sovereigns facing immediate refinancing or policy-credibility stress.

## Cross asset

Oil challenged an inflation-led explanation for Friday’s rates move. WTI fell 2.687% to $90.54 and Brent declined 2.0415% to $93.09. Both remained higher over the week, preserving the broader energy-pass-through risk, yet their daily retreat coincided with flat 10-year breakevens and sharply higher real yields. Payrolls and policy pricing, not a renewed crude spike, supplied the dominant Treasury impulse.

Equities transmitted the higher discount rate with exceptional force. The S&P 500 dropped 2.6445%, the Nasdaq 100 lost 4.7692%, and the semiconductor index fell 10.257%. The VIX jumped 39.6753% to 21.51. Long-duration technology valuations were most exposed to the real-yield rise, and concern over the scale and financing of AI investment deepened the adjustment.

The equity selloff was not a pure macro reaction. Technology shares had risen rapidly before Friday, creating room for profit-taking, and a disappointing Broadcom outlook, possible equity financing by Meta, bitcoin weakness and cash raising ahead of large offerings added pressure. Five of the S&P 500’s sectors still advanced as investors rotated toward defensive industries. Those details qualify the link between rates and stocks without erasing the common valuation channel.

The dollar and gold confirmed the real-rate direction. The dollar index gained to 100.07 as relative US policy expectations firmed, while gold futures fell 3.0989% to $4,337.10. Gold failed to provide conventional protection during the equity decline because the stronger dollar and higher real yields raised its opportunity cost. This combination tightened conditions for external borrowers and reinforced the EM fund loss.

Credit funds, equities and gold all weakened while Treasuries also sold off, leaving no clean haven beneficiary beyond the dollar. The absence of a shortest-bill disturbance and the successful financing cases argue against a generalized cash seizure. Cross assets instead describe an orderly but severe repricing of discount rates, amplified by crowded technology exposure and geopolitical uncertainty. Falling oil is the central unresolved counterforce because sustained relief there could weaken the inflation rationale for keeping policy restrictive.

## Forward Market Significance

The May US CPI release at 08:30 ET on June 10, followed by PPI and initial claims at 08:30 ET on June 11, will determine whether Friday’s real-yield-led flattening can persist. Consensus expects headline CPI to rise 0.5% on the month and 4.2% from a year earlier, after 0.6% and 3.8%, with core monthly inflation slowing to 0.3% from 0.4%. Broad strength across core components, followed by producer prices near or above the expected 0.6% headline and 0.5% core monthly increases, would validate the market’s year-end hike pricing and keep two-, five- and 10-year real yields under upward pressure. Softer underlying inflation alongside a rise in claims from the prior 225,000 would challenge the inference that stronger payrolls require a higher policy path and could reopen the duration relief that ended on Friday.

Treasury supply between June 8 and June 11 will show whether stable cash conditions extend into a heavier maturity mix. The $89 billion 13-week and $77 billion 26-week bill auctions begin the sequence, followed by $65 billion at six weeks, $50 billion at 52 weeks, a $58 billion three-year new issue, a $39 billion 10-year reopening and a $22 billion 30-year reopening. The observable signals are clearing levels against contemporaneous trading, bid-to-cover, bidder allocations, dealer retention and the behavior of bill yields around settlement. Broad demand and orderly cash rates would confirm that Friday was a macro and real-rate repricing with intact balance-sheet capacity. Weak coupon demand, rising dealer inventories or settlement pressure would reopen supply as an independent curve driver, with the 10- and 30-year sectors and rate-sensitive corporate issuance most exposed.

The global sequence centers on China’s May inflation data on June 10, the Bank of Canada decision later that day, the ECB decision on June 11 and India’s CPI release on June 12. China’s headline and factory-gate results relative to expectations of 1.4% and 3.5% year-on-year inflation will shape Asian duration, while a Canadian hold at 2.25% and the ECB’s choice around an expected quarter-point increase will reveal how energy-importing central banks balance inflation against weaker growth. An ECB signal limited to one move, contained Chinese pipeline pressure and Indian inflation near or below the 4.00% consensus would challenge the idea of a synchronized tightening cycle. Broader price acceleration or guidance pointing to repeated increases would confirm a higher global clearing rate, keeping European front ends, Asian currencies, long EM yields and the Treasury term structure under pressure.

section_summaries.market_read: The close re-established a policy-led, real-rate bear flattening without a cash-market rupture.
section_summaries.next_check: Persistence now depends on whether inflation data confirm the labor-led rise in real yields.
section_summaries.summary: Payroll strength overturned the prior session’s tentative relief, lifting real yields and concentrating Treasury losses in the front end. Falling oil and unchanged long-run inflation compensation pointed to a higher policy and discount-rate path, while orderly bills and completed financings kept the shock from becoming a broad funding break.

section_summaries.market_read: Front-end and belly sensitivity now dominate the Treasury regime, with inflation persistence decisive.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether core inflation breadth sustains year-end hike pricing and upward real-yield pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Losses declined with maturity as stronger hiring pushed the market toward a firmer Fed path, while real yields rose and long-run breakevens held steady. The curve therefore reflected front-loaded policy repricing rather than a distinct long-bond supply shock, although upcoming auctions will test absorption.

section_summaries.market_read: Credit weakness reflected duration and risk repricing, not a wholesale closure of financing access.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether new deals clear without greater concessions as Treasury supply absorbs balance sheet.
section_summaries.summary: Credit funds weakened with Treasuries and equities, but completed municipal and private deals showed financing remained available when collateral, guarantees, and loss allocation were explicit. That selectivity insulated funding from the broader real-yield shock, while exposed junior claims and indebted issuers retained refinancing risk.

section_summaries.market_read: Higher global clearing rates reinforce Treasury pressure, but weak growth caps uniform tightening.
section_summaries.next_check: The ECB’s June decision will show whether tightening stays limited or becomes a repeated sequence.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: India’s unchanged policy rate carried restraint through a higher inflation forecast, while Taiwan’s pipeline prices and Europe’s prospective tightening lifted global clearing-rate pressure. Weak euro-area growth and country-specific fiscal constraints limited synchronization, leaving front ends more vulnerable than duration across every market.

section_summaries.market_read: External funding conditions tightened, increasing differentiation by policy credibility and buffers.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether currency support and clearer fiscal terms stabilize Indonesia’s sovereign duration.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: Emerging-market debt faced simultaneous pressure from higher Treasury real yields and a stronger dollar, but country outcomes depended on inflation, fiscal supply, currency buffers, and policy credibility. Colombia and Indonesia showed distinct tightening channels, while China’s reserve stock offered a partial regional cushion.

section_summaries.market_read: Cross assets confirm higher real discount rates rather than a conventional flight to safety.
section_summaries.next_check: Sustained oil relief is the key observable counterforce to restrictive policy pricing.
section_summaries.summary: Falling oil and flat breakevens contradicted an energy-driven Treasury selloff, while the stronger dollar, weaker gold, and a technology-led equity decline confirmed a real-rate shock. Calm shortest bills and successful financings kept the episode orderly despite severe volatility and crowded-position amplification.

section_summaries.market_read: The regime now hinges on confirmation across prices, auction demand, and global policy.
section_summaries.next_check: The first decisive check is May CPI at 08:30 ET on June 10, followed by PPI and claims.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests will determine whether the bear flattening persists: US inflation and claims, Treasury supply absorption, and the global central-bank sequence. Broad price strength, weak coupon demand, or repeated tightening guidance would reinforce higher real and clearing rates; softer data and orderly auctions would reopen duration relief.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.19% (+8 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.36% (0 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 38 bp (-4 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 84 bp (-8 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Strong payrolls drove a real-yield-led Treasury selloff and sharper curve flattening
overall_summary.session_narrative: At Friday’s completed NY close, Treasury yields rose across maturities and the curve bear-flattened after payrolls beat forecasts and prior months were revised higher. Flat long-run breakevens and falling oil placed the adjustment in Fed pricing and real discount rates, while the stronger dollar and technology-led equity decline transmitted the shock across assets. Calm shortest bills and absorbed financings limited evidence of a generalized funding seizure.
overall_summary.summary: Labor resilience revived expectations for a tighter Fed path, lifting real yields and putting the front end under the most pressure. Funding conditions stayed orderly and completed financings showed selective access, limiting the move to a severe discount-rate repricing rather than a broad liquidity break.

watch_events.market_implication: Broad strength would pressure two-, five-, and 10-year real yields; softness would reopen relief.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad core strength with firm PPI, versus softer inflation and claims above 225,000.
watch_events.title: US inflation and claims test
watch_events.watch_line: May CPI, followed by PPI and claims, will test whether payroll-led policy repricing persists.

watch_events.market_implication: Weakness would pressure 10- and 30-year duration and issuance; strength preserves the macro reading.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad demand and orderly cash rates, versus weak bids or rising dealer inventories.
watch_events.title: Treasury supply absorption
watch_events.watch_line: Auction clearing, bidder mix, dealer retention, and settlement behavior will test capacity.

watch_events.market_implication: Hawkishness would lift global rates, euro front ends, Asian FX, long EM yields, and the Treasury curve.
watch_events.market_trigger: Contained Asian inflation and one limited ECB move, versus broader prices or repeated-hike guidance.
watch_events.title: Global policy and inflation sequence
watch_events.watch_line: China and India inflation plus Canadian and ECB decisions will test synchronized tightening.
